preamble: Lowering of the mid-front vowel [ε] to the low vowel [a] is a common sound change, often occurring before liquid consonants or as part of a general chain shift.

examples.note: Pre-rhotic lowering: [ɛ] → [a] before /r/ in non-rhotic dialects; the r vocalizes, lengthening and lowering the preceding vowel.
OE 'eo' diphthong simplified and underwent pre-rhotic lowering to [aː] in Modern English.
A-mutation: e → a in some Scandinavian forms; the specific outcome varies by dialect.

sources: Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction. MIT Press.
